title: Prepare an input clip
summary: Use this rail to set the stages that feed the full detector -> tracking -> calibration -> overlay path.
body: This workspace is for short, iterative setup on one broadcast clip at a time. The goal is to make the pipeline legible before you trust longer runs or downstream summaries.

A bad choice here usually fails downstream in a recognizable order: weak detection poisons tracking, weak tracking makes team separation noisy, and poor field registration makes the minimap or projection unreliable even if the boxes look reasonable.

title: Detector weights
summary: The detector decides which players, referees, and ball candidates exist at all. Every downstream stage depends on it.
body: In this repo, `soccana` is the football-specific detector path. It is preferred because the app is tuned around football broadcast footage rather than generic COCO-style scenes.

If the detector misses small distant players, confuses referees, or hallucinates crowd and board boxes, the tracker, team clustering, and ball coverage all inherit that damage. Detector swaps are useful only when they improve the overlay, not because they sound more advanced.

title: Player tracker
summary: The tracker turns frame-local detections into identities that survive motion, occlusion, and re-entry.
body: `bytetrack` is the fast baseline. It follows detections frame to frame, but it does not try to reconnect long identity gaps once a player disappears or the scene changes too aggressively.

`hybrid_reid` adds sparse appearance embeddings and a stitch pass. That gives you two identity layers: raw online IDs from the live tracker and canonical stitched IDs after the merge pass. Raw-vs-stitched counts are useful because they tell you whether the post-pass is cleaning up tracker churn or inventing over-confident merges.
Use the overlay and the raw-vs-stitched metrics together. A lower stitched ID count is only good if the video still looks coherent when players cross, crowd, or leave and re-enter the frame.

links.label: Long-term player tracking with graph hierarchies
Team-Aware Football Player Tracking with SAM

title: Include ball tracking
summary: Ball tracking is a supporting signal for match context, not the primary success condition for the pipeline.
body: The ball is small, fast, heavily occluded, and often ambiguous against socks, feet, or ad boards. Good player coverage can coexist with weak ball continuity.
Use ball output to judge whether a clip reads like real play and to sanity-check events, but do not let a weak ball trace distract you from fixing detection, tracking, or calibration first.

title: Confidence and IOU thresholds
summary: These values control how aggressively the runtime accepts detections and how permissive box matching is.
body: Higher confidence usually removes more false positives but can also erase small distant players and the ball. Lower confidence recovers recall, but it can flood the tracker with garbage detections that create churn.

IOU settings affect how readily overlapping boxes are treated as the same object. Threshold tuning is meaningful only after you inspect the overlay; the right values depend on the clip scale, crowding, and camera motion.

title: Automatic field registration
summary: Field calibration refreshes from pitch keypoints and quality gates. It can fail even when player boxes look healthy.
body: This pipeline refreshes pitch geometry from field keypoints, then accepts or rejects each candidate homography based on visibility and geometric quality. A run can have good detections and still lose minimap quality if calibration refreshes keep failing.

The review metrics around calibration success, stale recovery, gate rejects, and projection stages are there to show whether the minimap is using fresh geometry, coasting on stale state, or being blocked entirely.

Treat field registration as a live runtime component, not a cosmetic post-process. If calibration is weak, projected player positions and ball locations should be considered suspect even when image-space boxes look stable.

title: SoccerNet browsing and download
summary: SoccerNet is the benchmark and dataset surface behind much of the football CV ecosystem this app borrows from.
body: This section is not just a file picker. It is the bridge between the local app and current football-specific benchmark tasks such as action spotting, calibration, tracking, and game-state reconstruction.

Use it when you want current benchmark footage and labels without manually copying paths every session. `Labels-v2.json` remains important because several higher-level experiments and event-aligned analyses expect that structure.

title: Scan a local dataset folder
summary: Folder scan is a convenience layer for turning a local dataset tree into clickable clip and label choices.
body: Use this when your footage already lives on disk and you want the app to surface candidate videos and annotation files without manually retyping full paths.
This is operational help, not a model or benchmark concept, so there are no external references here.

title: Input Clip
summary: This panel is the raw source video. It is where you verify what the model is seeing before trusting any overlay.
body: Use the raw clip to compare against live preview and saved overlay outputs. If the source itself is confusing or cropped badly, every downstream artifact will be harder to interpret.
When something looks wrong in review, come back here first and confirm that the failure is not caused by the clip segment, framing, or source mismatch.

title: Live Inference Preview
summary: Live preview is the fast visual debugging surface for runtime behavior before you commit to a full saved run.
body: Use this mode to catch detector misses, ID churn, and calibration dropouts quickly. It is deliberately lighter than a full analysis run so you can iterate before waiting for saved outputs.
If the preview already looks weak, do not expect the saved review to rescue it. Fix the earliest broken stage first, then rerun.

title: Saved Run Review
summary: Review mode is where you judge the saved overlay, diagnostics, and exported metrics for one completed run.
body: The review surface should answer two questions quickly: did this run actually work, and which pipeline stage needs attention first if it did not.
Use the overlay as the primary artifact. Metrics and diagnostics are there to explain what the video is already showing, not to replace it with abstract counters.

title: Training Studio
summary: Training Studio scans a local detector dataset, runs a fine-tune, then lets you review and activate the resulting checkpoint for analysis.
body: This surface is detector-only on purpose. It keeps dataset validation, local fine-tuning, job review, and registry activation in one loop.
Use it when the stock `soccana` checkpoint does not fit your camera angle or footage domain. A successful run should improve the downstream analysis overlay, not just create a new file.

title: Active detector
summary: This is the checkpoint the analysis workspace will use whenever the detector selector stays on `soccana`.
body: A finished job is not live until its registry entry is activated. A checkpoint can exist on disk and still have no effect on analysis.
If analysis behavior changes unexpectedly, check this first. The visible detector selector can still say `soccana` while a custom checkpoint is active underneath it.

title: Dataset intake
summary: Dataset intake validates a local YOLO detection dataset before the studio enables fine-tuning.
body: The scan checks split structure, label files, class names, and the football class contract the analysis pipeline expects.
If intake is wrong, fix the dataset first. Hyperparameter tuning will not rescue missing splits or bad player, ball, or referee IDs.

title: Dataset path
summary: Point this at the detector dataset root, not a single image folder or an output directory.
body: The studio expects a YOLO-style dataset layout it can scan and turn into a run-local manifest. If the root is wrong, the scan fails before training starts.
The source dataset stays in place. The worker writes its own generated runtime YAML inside the training run folder.

title: Scan result
summary: The scan result tells you whether the dataset is clear to train and what blocked it if not.
body: A ready result means the studio found coherent splits, labels, and class mapping for the current detector workflow. A blocked result means training should wait.
Warnings still matter. They often explain fallbacks such as a generated validation list, which changes how much trust you should put in the run metrics.

title: Validation strategy
summary: Validation strategy tells you which validation split the job will use when it reports detector metrics.
body: If the dataset already has a real validation split, the studio keeps it. If it does not, the backend can generate a deterministic fallback inside the training run.
That choice affects how comparable mAP and recall numbers are across runs, so read it before you promote a checkpoint.

title: Class mapping
summary: Class mapping connects dataset class IDs to the football roles the analysis pipeline understands.
body: The studio needs player or keeper, ball, and referee IDs so a trained checkpoint can plug back into analysis without manual remapping.
A model can train cleanly and still be unusable here if those IDs are wrong.

title: Training family
summary: Training family keeps the studio scoped to the detector workflow that the app supports today.
body: Right now the active family is detector fine-tuning only. That keeps the form, jobs view, and registry aligned with the current backend.
If more model families arrive later, they should be added as distinct workflows instead of overloading this one.

title: Detector fine-tuning
summary: Detector fine-tuning starts a local job from football base weights and writes a candidate checkpoint plus review artifacts.
body: Each run captures the dataset scan, generated dataset manifest, logs, curves, metrics, and checkpoints under its training run directory.
A good run is one that improves the football overlay after activation, not one that simply finishes.

title: Base weights
summary: Base weights choose the starting checkpoint for the fine-tune.
body: Starting from `soccana` keeps the run anchored in football-specific player, ball, and referee behavior.
This choice is usually more important than small hyperparameter tweaks because it sets the model prior you are adapting.

title: Run name
summary: Run name should make jobs, registry entries, and artifact folders easy to recognize later.
body: Use something that captures what changed, such as camera domain, dataset version, or experiment intent.
You will see the same label in the jobs view and registry, so make it useful to your future self.

title: Training hyperparameters
summary: These controls shape how long the run trains, how much fits per step, and how aggressively the checkpoint adapts.
body: Epochs, image size, batch, workers, patience, and freeze trade runtime cost against adaptation strength and stability.
Start with a smaller smoke run when you are validating a new dataset. It is cheaper to catch bad labels or class mapping early.

title: Training device
summary: Device controls where the training job runs and what `auto` is likely to resolve to on this machine.
body: On Apple Silicon, `auto` usually resolves to MPS and gives the best local throughput when the backend supports it. CPU remains the safe fallback.
If a run is unstable or memory-bound, changing device is often the fastest way to get a reliable baseline.

title: Runtime behavior
summary: Runtime behavior covers what the worker writes while a job is running and what survives after it finishes.
body: The worker stores status, logs, metrics, curves, a generated dataset manifest, and checkpoint artifacts inside the run directory.
Those files are the audit trail the Jobs and Registry views rely on, so they still matter after training stops.

title: Training jobs
summary: Jobs is the main review surface for live progress, resolved device, metrics, and promotion readiness.
body: Use it to monitor queued or running work, inspect completed runs, and open the evidence behind a checkpoint before activation.
A finished job is only valuable if its logs, metrics, and artifacts explain what changed and whether the checkpoint is worth promoting.

title: Run artifacts
summary: Run artifacts are the files that let you audit or reuse a training job after the process exits.
body: Typical artifacts include the generated dataset manifest, plots, CSV outputs, logs, and checkpoint files.
If a run is worth keeping or promoting, these artifacts should be enough to reconstruct what data and settings produced it.

title: Detector registry
summary: The registry turns completed detector runs into selectable analysis behavior.
body: Each entry records the checkpoint path, training run, base weights, resolved device, class mapping, and key metrics.
Activation here controls what the analysis workspace uses for `soccana`, so compare entries carefully before switching.

title: Runtime profile
summary: Runtime profile shows which backend and devices this machine can actually use for local detector training.
body: It reflects the resolved training stack, the preferred device, and whether acceleration such as Apple Silicon MPS is available.
Use it to set expectations before you start a job. Device choice affects speed, memory pressure, and whether `auto` will resolve to MPS or CPU.

title: Detector training metrics
summary: These numbers tell you how the detector behaved on the validation split, and each one answers a different question.
body: Precision asks: when the detector says a box is a player, ball, or referee, how often is it actually right? High precision usually means fewer false positives, but it can still hide a detector that simply stays too conservative and misses hard cases.

Recall asks: of the real objects that should have been found, how many did the detector actually catch? High recall is valuable in football because distant players and the ball are easy to miss, but recall can improve for the wrong reason if the model starts throwing too many loose boxes.

mAP50 is the easier quality score: it rewards detections that overlap the ground truth at a 0.50 IoU threshold. mAP50-95 is the stricter score across harder IoU thresholds, so it is better for judging box quality and localization sharpness, not just whether the detector vaguely found the object.

Use these four together. A checkpoint with better recall but collapsing precision may flood tracking with garbage. A checkpoint with higher mAP50 but weak mAP50-95 may find the right object class while still drawing sloppy boxes. And none of these numbers are worth much if the validation split is weak. After comparing them here, verify the winner in the actual analysis overlay before activating it.

title: Epochs
summary: Epochs control how many full passes the detector makes over the training set before stopping or early-stopping out.
body: More epochs increase adaptation time, but they also increase the chance of spending compute on a dataset that was never worth trusting in the first place. In this studio, epochs should usually start small enough to prove the dataset contract and runtime path before you scale up.

Treat epochs as a cost-versus-evidence dial, not as a magical quality number. If a short smoke run already looks unstable, mis-mapped, or overconfident, raising epochs usually amplifies the wrong thing instead of fixing it.

title: Image size
summary: Image size sets the detector training resolution, which changes how much small-player and ball detail is preserved versus how expensive each step becomes.
body: Larger image sizes preserve more spatial detail, which matters in football because distant players and the ball are often tiny. The tradeoff is memory pressure, slower steps, and lower practical batch sizes on the same machine.
Use this control to match detector fidelity to your camera geometry. If your clips are wide-angle and distant, image size matters more than it would in a close-up sports dataset.

title: Batch
summary: Batch size controls how many training examples fit into each optimization step, which strongly affects memory use and training stability.
body: Higher batch sizes can smooth optimization and improve throughput, but only if the device can actually hold them. On local hardware, batch is often the first place where ambitious settings collide with memory limits.
If image size goes up, the batch size you can sustain usually comes down. Read these two controls together, not independently.

title: Workers
summary: Workers control how much host-side parallelism the data loader uses to feed training batches into the runtime.
body: This setting affects CPU load, disk access, and how smoothly the trainer can keep the accelerator fed. Too few workers can underutilize the training device. Too many can create unnecessary host contention on a local machine.

In practice, workers are part of the runtime profile, not just the model recipe. The best value depends on the dataset layout, storage speed, and what else the machine is doing while the run is active.

title: Patience
summary: Patience controls how long the run waits for improvement before early stopping decides that training is no longer paying off.
body: This protects you from wasting time on flat or regressing runs, but it can also stop too early if the validation signal is noisy or the dataset is small. The right value depends on how trustworthy the validation path is and how long you are willing to keep a local run alive before giving up.
Patience is not a replacement for good dataset quality. If the validation contract is weak, early stopping logic can look precise while still reacting to bad evidence.

title: Freeze
summary: Freeze determines how much of the pretrained detector stays fixed instead of adapting to the new dataset.
body: Freezing more layers preserves the original football prior and reduces how much the run can drift, but it also limits how far the detector can adapt to a new camera domain. Freezing fewer layers gives the run more freedom, at the cost of greater instability and a higher chance of wrecking useful pretrained behavior.
Use freeze when you want to control how aggressive the fine-tune is. It is one of the clearest knobs for deciding whether this run is a cautious domain adjustment or a deeper model rewrite.

title: Cache images
summary: Caching images trades extra local memory or disk pressure for faster data loading during the run.
body: This setting changes runtime behavior more than detector semantics. On some local setups it can make iteration much smoother by reducing repeated file reads, but on constrained machines it can simply move the bottleneck elsewhere.

Use caching when the dataset and machine actually benefit from it, not because it sounds like a free speedup. The right answer depends on storage, memory headroom, and the size of the dataset in play.
